limitation_statement: Current findings are strongest for graph-based inference under repulsive local couplings. They should not yet be generalized to all graph-structured inference domains.

ranked_domains.example_framing: neighbouring locations tend to share the same hidden label
ranked_domains.notes: Best first extension: flips local semantics while keeping graph-based inference structure intact.
Excellent first testbed, but strongly tied to exclusion dynamics.

ranked_domains.example_framing: linked agents tend to share opinions or affiliations
ranked_domains.notes: Intuitive and psychologically rich; especially useful for cluster-style approximations.

ranked_domains.example_framing: some relations are cooperative, others competitive
ranked_domains.notes: Theoretically rich, but too complex as the immediate next step.

ranked_domains.example_framing: infection or information spreads through edges
ranked_domains.notes: Preserves propagation logic, but task semantics and interface demands are more complex.

taxonomy_transfer.notes: Exact inference remains well-defined across all domains.
rho remains the main task-general structural attenuation parameter.
beta may become even more psychologically important when couplings vary in strength.
Target-centred rewrite remains meaningful across repulsive and attractive tasks.
Chunking local clusters may become more plausible under attractive couplings.

future_task_recommendation.reason: Best first extension: flips local semantics while keeping graph-based inference structure intact.

prospect_section_draft: Map-colouring remains an excellent first testbed for bounded graph inference, but it cannot on its own support domain-general claims about human structural approximation. The next task domain should preserve the graph-based inference interface while reversing the local coupling semantics from repulsive to attractive. A simple attractive graph-labelling task is therefore the cleanest first extension: it tests whether the same family taxonomy survives when shell-parity and local conflict counting cease to be the dominant cues.
